meta.description: Short moral sentences and adapted fable lines for beginning students

passages.title: Vita Brevis
passages.english: Life is short, art is long.
passages.notes: Adapted from Hippocrates via Latin tradition. Ars here means 'skill' or 'craft'.

passages.title: Aquila et Vulpes
passages.latin: Aquila in alta arbore sedet. Vulpes in terra ambulat. Aquila vocat; vulpes non respondet.
passages.english: An eagle sits in a tall tree. A fox walks on the ground. The eagle calls; the fox does not answer.
passages.notes: Adapted from Phaedrus I.28. Note the simple present tense throughout.

passages.title: Lupus et Agnus
passages.latin: Lupus et agnus ad rivum veniunt. Lupus agnum spectat. Agnus timet. Lupus malus est.
passages.english: A wolf and a lamb come to a stream. The wolf looks at the lamb. The lamb is afraid. The wolf is evil.
passages.notes: Adapted from Phaedrus I.1 (The Wolf and the Lamb). Classic moral fable.

passages.title: Cornix et Hydria
passages.latin: Cornix aquam videt sed aquam non potest bibere. Cornix lapides in hydriam mittit. Aqua surgit. Cornix bibit.
passages.english: A crow sees water but cannot drink the water. The crow puts stones into the jar. The water rises. The crow drinks.
passages.notes: Adapted from Phaedrus. Demonstrates resourcefulness — a classic Aesop theme.

passages.title: Labor et Sapientia
passages.latin: Labor salutem dat. Sapientia viam monstrat. Qui laborat et sapit beatus est.
passages.english: Work gives safety. Wisdom shows the way. He who works and is wise is blessed.
passages.notes: Original composition in Phaedran style for review lesson.
